courthouse.name: Atoka County Courthouse
courthouse.hours: 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. Monday through Friday
courthouse.judicialDistrict: 25th Judicial District (covers Atoka and Coal counties)

majorEmployers.name: Mack Alford Correctional Center
majorEmployers.notes: Medium-security Oklahoma Department of Corrections facility opened in 1973; also includes a minimum-security unit with approximately 260 inmates

majorEmployers.name: INCA Community Services
majorEmployers.notes: Community action agency providing Head Start, senior services, and community development programs

majorEmployers.name: Atoka County Medical Center
majorEmployers.notes: Critical access hospital affiliated with the Veterans Health Administration

majorEmployers.name: Atoka Public Schools
majorEmployers.notes: Serves approximately 941 students across elementary, middle, and high school

majorEmployers.name: Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma
majorEmployers.notes: Tribal government services, casino operations, health clinics, and community programs

uniqueFacts: Atoka County was named for Captain Atoka, a Choctaw warrior who led a removal party from Mississippi to Indian Territory and was a co-signer of the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek in 1830
The county contains the only designated Confederate cemetery in Oklahoma, located at the Atoka Museum and Civil War Cemetery
St. Patrick's Catholic Church in Atoka, established in 1872 by Father Michael Smyth, was the first Catholic congregation in present-day Oklahoma
The Battle of Middle Boggy Depot took place in the county on February 13, 1864, during the Civil War
Atoka County is home to McGee Creek State Park, a popular trophy bass lake destination, and Boggy Depot State Park, a historic Butterfield Overland Mail stagecoach stop
processServingNotes: Atoka County is within the 25th Judicial District alongside Coal County; process serving is handled through the District Court Clerk at the Atoka County Courthouse
As part of Choctaw Nation reservation lands, certain legal matters involving tribal members may fall under Choctaw Nation tribal court jurisdiction
The Mack Alford Correctional Center in Stringtown is a secure facility; process servers should coordinate with facility staff and follow DOC protocols when serving incarcerated individuals
heroLead: Nestled in the Ouachita Mountains of southeastern Oklahoma, Atoka County blends rich Choctaw heritage with outdoor recreation across 990 square miles of forests, lakes, and rolling hills. Home to the historic Butterfield Overland Mail route, the only Confederate cemetery in Oklahoma, and country music legend Reba McEntire's hometown of Chockie, Atoka County offers a unique slice of frontier history. From serving legal papers at the 200 E. Court Street courthouse to navigating the rural roads of Choctaw Country, trust Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832 or visit /contact for reliable process serving throughout Atoka County.

faqs.question: Where is the Atoka County Courthouse located?
faqs.answer: The Atoka County Courthouse is located at 200 E. Court Street in Atoka, Oklahoma 74525. The courthouse is open Monday through Friday from 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. The District Court Clerk's office handles civil and criminal filings for the 25th Judicial District. For assistance with process serving in Atoka County, call Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What tribal jurisdiction applies in Atoka County?
faqs.answer: Atoka County is located within the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma reservation boundaries. The Choctaw Nation exercises jurisdiction over tribal members and trust lands within the county. Process servers should be aware that cases involving enrolled Choctaw Nation members may require coordination with Choctaw Nation tribal courts depending on the nature of the legal matter. The Choctaw Nation District 10 office serves Atoka County.

faqs.question: What is the population and geography of Atoka County?
faqs.answer: Atoka County has a population of approximately 14,600 residents as of 2024 and covers about 990 square miles in southeastern Oklahoma. The county is part of the region known as Choctaw Country. Major communities include Atoka (county seat), Caney, Stringtown, and Tushka. The terrain varies from the Ouachita Mountains in the east to rolling Sandstone Hills and Coastal Plains in the west.

faqs.question: Who are the major employers in Atoka County?
faqs.answer: Major employers include the Mack Alford Correctional Center (Oklahoma Department of Corrections), INCA Community Services, Atoka County Medical Center, Atoka Public Schools, and the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma. The correctional center alone employs over 500 staff. Cattle ranching, timber, and limestone quarrying also remain significant to the local economy. For business-related process serving, call (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What historic sites are in Atoka County?
faqs.answer: Atoka County is rich in history. Notable sites include the Atoka Museum and Civil War Cemetery (the only designated Confederate cemetery in Oklahoma), Boggy Depot State Park (a historic Butterfield Overland Mail stagecoach stop and original Choctaw settlement), and St. Patrick's Catholic Church (the oldest Catholic parish in Indian Territory, founded in 1872). The county also has the oldest Masonic Lodge and Order of the Eastern Star chapter in Oklahoma.

faqs.question: What should process servers know about serving papers in Atoka County?
faqs.answer: Process servers in Atoka County must operate within the 25th Judicial District procedures. Papers should be filed through the District Court at the Atoka County Courthouse, 200 E. Court Street, Atoka, OK 74525, phone (580) 889-3565. The county's rural nature and large land area require efficient routing. Stringtown, home to the Mack Alford Correctional Center, requires special coordination for inmate service. For professional process serving throughout Atoka County, contact Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.
